Google Dataset

  • Released by Google in \(2002\) as part of the Google Programming Contest
  • Made publicly available through the Stanford Network Analysis Project (SNAP)
  • 2002: aftermath of the dotcom bubble effecting the internet space

Dataset Description

Basic Network Elements of the web-Google Network
Property Value
Nodes 875713
Edges 5105039
Directionality Yes
Weighted No
Self-loops No
Density 6.7e-06
Mean Degree 11.66
Max Degree 6353

Objectives

  • Generate representative subgraphs of the Google Web graph using random walk sampling to deal with sparsity limitations of induced subgraphs
  • Look into sampling bias by comparing degree distributions between the sampled subgraph and full network using the Kolmpgorov-Smirnov test
  • Visualize the network structure of our subgraphs with the Fruchterman-Reingold, Kamada-Kawai, and Multidimensional Scaling (MDS) layout to reveal structural and connectivity patterns
  • Compute and interpret network-level metrics (i.e. density, reciprocity, dyad census, transitivity, diameter, and mean distance) to capture the overall structure of the web graph
  • Compute and interpret node-level metrics (i.e. in-degree, out-degree, betweenness centrality, closness centrality, and local transitivity) to identify influential web pages within the network
  • Detect and compare communities using Fast-Greedy, Louvain, Walktrap algorithms and evaluate their results using molecularity scores and community sizes
  • Examine the adjacency matrix of each subgraphs in their original and reordered form to confirm the presence of community strucutre and identify structural patterns.

induced_subgraph() Implemenation

## IGRAPH d2a5517 DN-- 200 1 -- 
## + attr: name (v/c)
## + edge from d2a5517 (vertex names):
## [1] 860469->343525
## IGRAPH 33b26c8 DN-- 200 0 -- 
## + attr: name (v/c)
## + edges from 33b26c8 (vertex names):
## IGRAPH 3e08cab DN-- 200 0 -- 
## + attr: name (v/c)
## + edges from 3e08cab (vertex names):
## IGRAPH a22631e DN-- 200 1 -- 
## + attr: name (v/c)
## + edge from a22631e (vertex names):
## [1] 203345->306718

Random Walk Sampling Code

set.seed(123)

subgraphs <- list()

for(i in 1:4){
  start_node <- sample(V(graph), 1)
  walk <- random_walk(graph,
                      start = start_node,
                      steps = 500,
                      mode = "all")
  nodes_sampled <- unique(walk)
  nodes_sampled <- nodes_sampled[1:200]
  subgraphs[[i]] <- induced_subgraph(graph, nodes_sampled)
}

Checking Random Walk Sampling

Sampling Bias Assessment of Random Walk Subgraphs
Subgraph Nodes Edges Mean Degree Median Degree KS P-Value
Subgraph 1 200 957 9.57 7 0.0000337
Subgraph 2 200 970 9.70 5 0.0062651
Subgraph 3 200 842 8.42 6 0.0000047
Subgraph 4 200 1608 16.08 12 0.0000000

Subgraph 4 Visuals

Metrics - Structure & Connectivity

Metric Value
Density 0.0404
Centralization 0.1460
Avg Path Length 5.2920
Reciprocity 0.4280
Transitivity 0.6700
  • Low density confirms sparse web graph structure
  • Low Centralization shows lack of dominant hub in subgraph
  • Reciprocity reflects observations of close-knit communities
  • Transitivity indicates high degree of local clustering

Community Detection

  • Fast Greedy, Louvain, and Walktrap
Subgraph Algorithm N_communities Modularity_Q Max_Comm_Size
4 Fast Greedy 7 0.7436 40
4 Louvain 8 0.7396 38
4 Walktrap 12 0.7357 38

Community Detection

  • Walktrap detects more communities than other algorithms
    • Fast greedy and Louvain typically struggle to see smaller communities
    • Implies a hierarchical/subcommunity structure
  • Stable results with modularity of around 0.7 or higher
  • Largest community is less than a quarter of entire network size

Subgraph 4 Community Detection Visualization

Adjacency Matrix

  • 200x200 matrix where each cell = 1 if a hyperlink exists between two pages
  • Original ordering: edges appear scattered, no visible structure
  • Goal: reorder nodes to reveal hidden patterns

Adjacency Matrix: Reordered by Community

  • Nodes reordered by Louvain community membership
  • Diagonal blocks emerge — pages in the same community densely hyperlink each other
  • Subgraph 4 (1,608 edges) shows the clearest block structure
  • Off-diagonal sparsity confirms pages from different communities rarely link directly

Adjacency Matrix Reordered

Reordering Nodes by Connected Component

  • All 200 nodes in each subgraph belong to a single connected component
  • Reordering by component yields identical results to community reordering
  • Two patterns visible in reordered matrices:
    • L-shape: central hub page linking to many communities (e.g. Wikipedia-like pages)
    • Solid block: tightly interconnected pages, likely subpages of the same site

Conclusion

  • Random Walk sampling allowed us to analyze meaningful locally connected regions for our sparse Google web graph

  • Our sampled network consistently showed strong community structure, where webpages cluster into tightly connected groups

  • High reciprocity and transitivity indicatet that webpages frequently link within their own communities

  • Community detection algorithms produces high modularity (\(\approx 0.7+\)), confirming well-defined communities

  • Walktrap detected more communities than Louvian and Fast-Greedy - hierarchical subcommunities within larger clusters

  • Reordered adjacency matrices revealed clear block-diagonal patterns - hyperlinks are concentrated within topical clusters rather than randomly distributed

  • Occasional hub-like pages connect multiple communities - bridges between different topics

Future Direction

  • Analyze larger sampled subgraphs to capture more global network structure
  • Compare different network sampling methods to reduce sampling bias
  • Explore temporal web datasets to study how web structure evolved over time

Thank You
